synopsis: The book presents retirement as a transition that requires more than financial preparation, organizing its guidance around passion, purpose, and planning. Drawing on retirees’ experiences, it offers practical prompts for building routines, relationships, activities, and goals that can make later life active and meaningful.

sources.title: How To Retire and Not Die | Gary Sirak

synopsis: A university chief investment officer oversees a six-billion-dollar endowment while fielding pitches from hedge funds, venture capitalists, and other money managers. As his assumptions about investing, professional judgment, and the choices that shaped his life begin to unravel, the novel examines finance, institutional ambition, and personal doubt.

sources.title: The Counting House | University of New Orleans Press

synopsis: Vaclav Smil explains the material foundations of modern civilization through energy, food, industrial materials, globalization, risk, and environmental change. Using quantitative evidence, he challenges both catastrophic and techno-optimistic narratives and argues that effective decisions about decarbonization and the future require an accurate understanding of physical constraints.

sources.title: How the World Really Works | Penguin Random House

synopsis: The book argues that psychological suffering often comes from treating every thought as true and continuing cycles of compulsive thinking. It offers a concise framework for noticing thoughts without identifying with them, reducing overthinking, and approaching circumstances with greater clarity and emotional freedom.

sources.title: Don’t Believe Everything You Think | Simon & Schuster

synopsis: Eric Ries argues that companies often abandon their missions because growth changes their ownership, incentives, accountability, and decision-making systems, not simply because leaders become unethical. He presents governance as a design problem and offers approaches intended to help organizations remain durable, profitable, and aligned with their founding purposes.

sources.title: Incorruptible | Simon & Schuster

synopsis: This history traces Apple from its founding by Steve Jobs and Steve Wozniak through the leadership struggles and strategic failures that nearly destroyed the company. Michael Malone examines how inventive products, charismatic personalities, weak management, and an insular corporate culture combined to produce both wide influence and recurring turmoil.

sources.title: Infinite Loop | Open Library

synopsis: Michael Moritz recounts Apple’s first decade alongside the early lives and partnership of Steve Jobs and Steve Wozniak. This revised edition follows the company from a small Silicon Valley venture through rapid growth and adds later perspective on Jobs’s return and Apple’s transformation into a global technology business.

sources.title: Return to the Little Kingdom | Google Books

synopsis: The book chronicles the decades-long effort to design, fund, construct, and operate the 200-inch Hale Telescope at Palomar Observatory. Set against Depression-era America, it follows the scientists, engineers, craftsmen, donors, and institutions whose technical disputes and persistence turned an unprecedented astronomical instrument into a landmark of big science.

sources.title: The Perfect Machine | Apple Books

synopsis: Doris Kearns Goodwin follows Abraham Lincoln’s rise over prominent rivals William Seward, Salmon Chase, and Edward Bates, then his decision to bring them into his cabinet. The biography examines how Lincoln managed competing personalities and political factions while leading the Union through civil war and moving the country toward abolition.

sources.title: Team of Rivals | Simon & Schuster

synopsis: Bill Perkins argues that money should be used to maximize meaningful experiences across a lifetime rather than accumulated without a clear purpose. He introduces tools such as time-bucketing experiences, considering health and age, giving wealth earlier, and managing longevity risk to align spending with the periods when it can create the most value.

sources.title: Die With Zero | Official Book Site

synopsis: David Epstein argues that well-chosen constraints can improve creativity, collaboration, decision-making, and satisfaction by narrowing attention and forcing people to develop new approaches. Through examples from work, science, sports, and the arts, he examines how limits can counter excessive choice and help individuals and organizations identify productive edges.

sources.title: Inside the Box | Penguin Random House

synopsis: Eric Berger chronicles SpaceX’s development of the Falcon 9 from an expendable launcher into a rocket whose first stage could land and fly again. The account follows the engineering failures, financial pressures, launch campaigns, and internal decisions that made routine reuse possible and reshaped the economics and pace of orbital spaceflight.

sources.title: Reentry | Simon & Schuster

synopsis: Robert Higgs examines how wars, depressions, and other national emergencies expand the size, scope, and authority of the United States government. He develops a ratchet model in which emergency powers recede only partly after each crisis, while changed institutions and public beliefs make the remaining growth durable.

sources.title: Crisis and Leviathan | Independent Institute

synopsis: Michael Riordan and Lillian Hoddeson reconstruct the scientific and institutional path to the transistor, centering on John Bardeen, Walter Brattain, and William Shockley at Bell Laboratories. They connect solid-state physics, wartime research, corporate laboratories, patents, manufacturing, and personal rivalries to the device’s evolution into microelectronics and the modern information industry.

sources.title: Crystal Fire | Google Books

synopsis: Sebastian Mallaby traces Demis Hassabis’s path from chess prodigy and game designer to the cofounding of DeepMind and leadership of Google’s artificial intelligence efforts. The biography uses his career to examine advances in machine learning, the contest for research talent and computing power, and debates over control, safety, and superintelligence.

sources.title: The Infinity Machine | Penguin Random House

synopsis: Salvatore Basile follows the development of air conditioning from early cooling schemes and Willis Carrier’s humidity-control equipment through its gradual commercial and domestic adoption. The history shows how mechanical cooling changed factories, theaters, architecture, migration, work, leisure, and settlement across the United States while creating new patterns of energy use.

sources.title: Cool | Fordham University Press

synopsis: Dan Wang contrasts China’s engineering-centered state with an American system shaped heavily by lawyers, procedures, and limits on construction. Drawing on years in China, he examines the country’s manufacturing and infrastructure capacity, the political and human costs of rapid execution, and what each nation’s strengths and failures reveal about governing for the future.

sources.title: Breakneck | W. W. Norton

synopsis: Lloyd Blankfein recounts his path from Brooklyn public housing and an early legal career to commodities trading and three decades at Goldman Sachs. The memoir covers the firm’s partnership culture, public offering, leadership succession, financial innovation, the 2008 crisis, regulatory conflict, and the decisions he faced as chief executive.

sources.title: Streetwise | Penguin Random House Higher Education

synopsis: Bill Gurley argues that a satisfying career begins with identifying an absorbing pursuit and deliberately building the skills and relationships needed to follow it. Combining his own path through technology and venture capital with profiles from several industries, he discusses risk, persistence, mentorship, setbacks, and avoiding conventional career choices that produce regret.

sources.title: Runnin’ Down a Dream | Penguin Random House

synopsis: Brian Jay Jones traces George Lucas from his California childhood and film-school experiments through the creation of Lucasfilm, Star Wars, and Indiana Jones. The biography examines his pursuit of creative independence, work in visual effects and digital filmmaking, business decisions, collaborations, family life, and the setbacks and conflicts surrounding his most influential projects.

sources.title: George Lucas | Hachette Book Group

synopsis: This collection gathers thirty-five pieces Freeman Dyson wrote across six decades, beginning with a childhood science-fiction story and ending with reflections on Earth as a living system. The essays, lectures, reviews, and recollections range across physics, astronomy, scientific institutions, nuclear weapons, space exploration, environmental questions, literature, and portraits of other scientists.

sources.title: From Eros to Gaia | Penguin Random House

synopsis: Edited by Peter Kaufman, this volume collects Charlie Munger’s speeches, lectures, public commentary, and biographical material in one illustrated work. Its recurring subjects include multidisciplinary mental models, incentives, cognitive biases, business and investment judgment, learning, ethics, and the habits Munger believed support clearer decisions and a useful life.

sources.title: Poor Charlie’s Almanack | PCA Publications

synopsis: Ben Rich recounts the history and operating methods of Lockheed’s secret Skunk Works division, including the U-2, SR-71, and stealth aircraft programs. His memoir combines engineering and management decisions with accounts from designers, intelligence officials, and pilots, showing how small teams delivered experimental military aircraft under severe technical, political, budgetary, and security constraints.

sources.title: Skunk Works | Hachette Book Group

synopsis: Brian Potter examines how production processes become faster, cheaper, more reliable, and less demanding of labor and materials. Using cases from steel, light bulbs, penicillin, automobiles, semiconductors, wind turbines, and shipping, he identifies mechanisms behind efficiency gains and considers why similar cost improvements have been difficult in housing, medicine, and education.

sources.title: The Origins of Efficiency | Stripe Press

synopsis: Eliezer Yudkowsky and Nate Soares argue that a machine more capable than humans would pursue learned objectives rather than reliably adopt human values. They explain opacity, goal misalignment, strategic behavior, and power-seeking in advanced artificial intelligence, present a possible extinction scenario, and call for coordinated action to stop the development of superhuman systems.

sources.title: If Anyone Builds It, Everyone Dies | Hachette Book Group

synopsis: Written for ambitious students and recent graduates, the book combines ideas and exercises intended to connect high performance with personal satisfaction. It covers inspiration, vision, responsibility, habits, study, health, communication, networking, mentoring, and leisure, presenting these areas as parts of a practical framework for pursuing excellence across different fields.

sources.title: Racing Towards Excellence | Columbia Center on Capitalism and Society

synopsis: Tae Kim traces Nvidia from its 1993 founding and early focus on graphics chips through repeated technical and commercial crises to its central role in artificial intelligence computing. Based on interviews with Jensen Huang, cofounders, investors, and employees, the history examines the company’s product bets, management practices, internal culture, and creation of new markets.

sources.title: The Nvidia Way | W. W. Norton

synopsis: Drawing on journals kept since adolescence, Matthew McConaughey recounts episodes from his Texas childhood, travels, relationships, family life, and acting career. He mixes memoir with poems, prayers, photographs, and personal maxims, using the idea of traffic lights to describe recognizing opportunities, responding to setbacks, and finding direction in changing circumstances.

sources.title: Greenlights | Penguin Random House

synopsis: This volume collects twenty-eight Slate Star Codex essays written in 2013 and 2014 on philosophy, politics, economics, religion, science, culture, history, mathematics, and society. The pieces use arguments, dialogues, stories, reviews, and satire to explore subjects including coordination failures, online outrage, political ideology, artificial intelligence, ethics, and human motivation.

sources.title: Slate Star Codex Essays: Volume I | Russo’s Books

synopsis: This second volume collects thirty-five Slate Star Codex essays written between 2015 and 2017 across philosophy, politics, economics, religion, science, culture, history, mathematics, and society. Through explanatory essays, dialogues, fiction, and book reviews, the pieces address Bayesian reasoning, psychotherapy, political discourse, institutions, altered states, morality, and the interpretation of evidence.

sources.title: Slate Star Codex Essays: Volume II | Barnes & Noble
